picks.reason: Ryan Gusto carries a small-sample flag and his aggregate SIERA is 4.30 against Zack Wheeler’s elite 2.90 mark, but the market prices Philadelphia correctly at -205. My adjustments for bullpen usage and a slight offensive edge for Miami only close the gap to about two points, well short of the 4-point gate.

Both starters carry small-sample flags—Mitch Spence has just 39 innings of aggregate data and Andrew Alvarez only 28—making a reliable pitching gap impossible to estimate. Without a trustworthy run-prevention edge, the projected win probability sits inside the market’s implied range.

Chase Burns owns a 2.98 SIERA and 24.5% K-BB% over more than 100 innings, while Tobias Myers brings a 5.36 SIERA and 4.9% K-BB%. I estimate Cincinnati’s true win probability at 61.2%, creating a 4.5-point edge over the -131 implied probability of 56.7%.
picks.data_gap: Myers has no L14 data, so recent degradation cannot be ruled out.

picks.reason: San Diego’s starter is listed as TBD, which is an automatic pass under the data-integrity rules. No edge can be estimated without a confirmed starting pitcher.

Michael Lorenzen and Shota Imanaga are nearly identical in aggregate SIERA, and while the Cubs hold a large platoon advantage at the plate the market already prices them at -195. The resulting gap is only about two points, so there is no actionable edge.
Minnesota’s starter is TBD, forcing an automatic pass regardless of MacKenzie Gore’s profile. No probability can be assigned without confirmed pitching data.

Troy Melton carries a small-sample flag, and his aggregate SIERA is nearly identical to Kai-Wei Teng’s 4.38 mark. With minimal pitching separation and a modest Houston offensive edge, my estimated probability tracks the -125 market line too closely.

Walbert Ureña and Ryne Nelson are separated by just 0.03 runs in aggregate SIERA, making the rotation essentially a wash. Los Angeles holds a slight offensive advantage, but that only narrows Arizona’s implied probability rather than flipping an edge.

Jared Jones is small-sample flagged and J.T. Ginn is only marginally better in SIERA, while Sutter Health Park’s extreme hitter-friendly environment inflates variance. I avoid side bets in high-variance parks without a clear run-prevention advantage, though the conditions nudge me toward the Over.

Nick Martinez and Eric Lauer are virtual equals in aggregate SIERA, and while Los Angeles holds a bullpen-availability edge and a platoon advantage at the plate, the adjustments only produce a gap of about one and a half points against a -158 line. That falls short of the required 4-point gate.
